content.text: this repo needs contributing guidelines / somethign for first time contributors..what should it say based on how this repo is set up

I’ll take a quick look at the repo shape and the existing docs/scripts so the guidance matches what a first-time contributor would actually see here. Then I’ll suggest concrete CONTRIBUTING content rather than generic open-source wallpaper.

The repo is tiny and intentionally skill-first: just the skill directories plus README/tutorial/package metadata. I also see local untracked files already here (`TUTORIAL.md`, `.claude/worktrees/`), so I’ll avoid treating the tree as clean or proposing anything that assumes npm build machinery exists.

input.cmd: for f in skills/*/SKILL.md; do printf '%s\n' "$f"; sed -n '1,80p' "$f"; done

content.text: There are a few compatibility files hidden from the first pass: Claude/Codex/Cursor plugin metadata, Gemini extension metadata, and an OpenCode plugin shim. That means the guidelines should teach people to update more than `skills/foo/SKILL.md` when a change affects installation or cross-agent behavior.

I’m going to add a focused `CONTRIBUTING.md` rather than just describe it. The key bits are: skill metadata is part of the public API here, validation is mostly “can installers discover the skills?”, and docs/README/plugin version metadata need to move together when behavior changes.

input.input:
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: CONTRIBUTING.md
+# Contributing to Entire Skills
+
+Thanks for helping improve Entire Skills. This repo is intentionally small: each
+skill lives in `skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`, with a few plugin and extension
+metadata files that make the same skills installable across different agents.
+
+## Repo layout
+
+- `skills/*/SKILL.md` contains the actual skill instructions.
+- `README.md` describes the published skills and installation paths.
+- `TUTORIAL.md` gives a first-use walkthrough.
+- `.codex-plugin/`, `.claude-plugin/`, `.cursor-plugin/`, `.agents/`,
+  `.opencode/`, and `gemini-extension.json` provide agent-specific metadata.
+- `package.json` is lightweight package metadata for plugin consumers.
+
+There is no application build system in this repo. Most changes are Markdown,
+JSON metadata, or a small plugin shim.
+
+## Before you start
+
+1. Fork or branch from `main`.
+2. Read the existing skill closest to the one you want to change.
+3. Keep the change focused. A skill should teach an agent one repeatable
+   workflow, not become a general manual.
+
+## Adding or changing a skill
+
+Each skill must live at:
+
+```text
+skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md
+```
+
+Every `SKILL.md` needs YAML front matter with at least:
+
+```yaml
+---
+name: skill-name
+description: A short description of when an agent should use this skill.
+---
+```
+
+The `name` and `description` fields are required for skill discovery. Missing
+metadata can cause installers to skip the skill.
+
+Good skill instructions should:
+
+- say when to use the skill and when not to use it
+- give the agent a concrete workflow
+- name required commands, files, and expected inputs
+- describe failure modes and what the agent should tell the user
+- avoid secrets, private transcripts, or user-specific local paths
+- stay focused on durable behavior instead of one-off session details
+
+If you add, remove, rename, or materially change a skill, update `README.md` and
+`TUTORIAL.md` when the public list, examples, or walkthrough changes.
+
+## Agent compatibility
+
+This repo supports several skill/plugin discovery paths. If your change affects
+installation, naming, versioning, or package metadata, check whether these files
+also need updates:
+
+- `package.json`
+- `gemini-extension.json`
+- `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`
+- `.claude-plugin/plugin.json`
+- `.cursor-plugin/plugin.json`
+- `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json`
+- `.opencode/INSTALL.md`
+- `.opencode/plugins/entire.js`
+
+Keep version numbers and descriptions consistent across metadata files when you
+are preparing a release.
+
+## Validation
+
+Before opening a PR, run the checks that match your change.
+
+Validate JSON files:
+
+```bash
+node -e 'for (const f of ["package.json","gemini-extension.json",".codex-plugin/plugin.json",".claude-plugin/plugin.json",".cursor-plugin/plugin.json",".agents/plugins/marketplace.json"]) JSON.parse(require("fs").readFileSync(f, "utf8"))'
+```
+
+Check that the skills installer can discover the repo skills:
+
+```bash
+npx -y skills@latest add . --list --yes
+```
+
+If you changed the OpenCode plugin shim, check its syntax:
+
+```bash
+node --check .opencode/plugins/entire.js
+```
+
+For Markdown-only changes, read the rendered Markdown or preview it in GitHub
+before submitting.
+
+## Pull requests
+
+In your PR, include:
+
+- what changed
+- why it changed
+- which skills or install paths are affected
+- what validation you ran
+
+If you changed skill behavior, include a short example prompt that demonstrates
+the new or updated behavior.
+
+## Style
+
+- Prefer concise, direct instructions over long explanations.
+- Use exact commands when the workflow depends on a command.
+- Keep examples generic unless a specific tool or repo is required.
+- Do not paste raw session transcripts into skills.
+- Do not commit local agent state, logs, temp files, or machine-specific config.
+
+If you are unsure whether a change belongs in a skill, the README, or the
+tutorial, put repeatable agent behavior in the skill, public summary in the
+README, and step-by-step first-use guidance in the tutorial.
*** End Patch

content.text: The first pass is in place. I’m doing quick doc/metadata validation now, mostly to catch silly JSON breakage and to see whether the recommended installer discovery command is truthful in this repo.

# Contributing to Entire Skills

Thanks for helping improve Entire Skills. This repo is intentionally small: each
skill lives in `skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`, with a few plugin and extension
metadata files that make the same skills installable across different agents.

## Repo layout

- `skills/*/SKILL.md` contains the actual skill instructions.
- `README.md` describes the published skills and installation paths.
- `TUTORIAL.md` gives a first-use walkthrough.
- `.codex-plugin/`, `.claude-plugin/`, `.cursor-plugin/`, `.agents/`,
  `.opencode/`, and `gemini-extension.json` provide agent-specific metadata.
- `package.json` is lightweight package metadata for plugin consumers.

There is no application build system in this repo. Most changes are Markdown,
JSON metadata, or a small plugin shim.

## Before you start

Fork the repo:

```bash
gh repo fork entireio/skills --clone
cd skills
```

Create a branch from `main`:

```bash
git checkout main
git pull origin main
git checkout -b add-my-skill
```

## Adding or changing a skill

Each skill must live at:

```text
skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md
```

Every `SKILL.md` needs YAML front matter with at least:

```yaml
---
name: skill-name
description: A short description of when an agent should use this skill.
---
```

The `name` and `description` fields are required for skill discovery. Missing
metadata can cause installers to skip the skill.

Before writing, read one existing skill that is similar to your change. For
example, if you are adding a provenance skill, read `skills/explain/SKILL.md` or
`skills/what-happened/SKILL.md` first so the new instructions match the repo's
style and level of detail.

Good skill instructions should:

- say when to use the skill and when not to use it
- give the agent a concrete workflow
- name required commands, files, and expected inputs
- describe failure modes and what the agent should tell the user
- avoid secrets, private transcripts, or user-specific local paths
- stay focused on one repeatable workflow instead of becoming a general manual

If you add, remove, rename, or materially change a skill, update `README.md` and
`TUTORIAL.md` when the public list, examples, or walkthrough changes.

## Agent compatibility

This repo supports several skill/plugin discovery paths. If your change affects
installation, naming, versioning, or package metadata, check whether these files
also need updates:

- `package.json`
- `gemini-extension.json`
- `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`
- `.claude-plugin/plugin.json`
- `.cursor-plugin/plugin.json`
- `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json`
- `.opencode/INSTALL.md`
- `.opencode/plugins/entire.js`

Keep version numbers and descriptions consistent across metadata files when you
are preparing a release.

## Validation

Before opening a PR, run the checks that match your change.

Validate JSON files:

```bash
node -e 'for (const f of ["package.json","gemini-extension.json",".codex-plugin/plugin.json",".claude-plugin/plugin.json",".cursor-plugin/plugin.json",".agents/plugins/marketplace.json"]) JSON.parse(require("fs").readFileSync(f, "utf8"))'
```

Check that the skills installer can discover the repo skills:

```bash
npx -y skills@latest add . --list --yes
```

If you changed the OpenCode plugin shim, check its syntax:

```bash
node --check .opencode/plugins/entire.js
```

For Markdown-only changes, read the rendered Markdown or preview it in GitHub
before submitting.

## Pull requests

In your PR, include:

- what changed
- why it changed
- which skills or install paths are affected
- what validation you ran

If you changed skill behavior, include a short example prompt that demonstrates
the new or updated behavior.

## Style

- Prefer concise, direct instructions over long explanations.
- Use exact commands when the workflow depends on a command.
- Keep examples generic unless a specific tool or repo is required.
- Do not paste raw session transcripts into skills.
- Do not commit local agent state, logs, temp files, or machine-specific config.

If you are unsure whether a change belongs in a skill, the README, or the
tutorial, put repeatable agent behavior in the skill, public summary in the
README, and step-by-step first-use guidance in the tutorial.

what does this mean for users? do they need to know this? It's okay if they do..but i want to know from u

There is no application build system in this repo. Most changes are Markdown,
JSON metadata, or a small plugin shim.

It means: contributors should not expect the usual “install dependencies, run the app, run tests/build” workflow. This repo is mostly instructions and metadata, so validation is more about “does the skill show up?” and “is the JSON valid?” than “does the app compile?”

I think it’s useful, but maybe too implementation-y where it is. For first-time contributors, I’d rewrite it as:

```md
You do not need to build an app to contribute here. Most changes are edits to skill instructions, README content, or JSON metadata.
```

That gives them the practical takeaway without sounding like internal repo anatomy.

# Contributing to Entire Skills

Thanks for helping improve Entire Skills. This repo is intentionally small: each
skill lives in `skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`, with a few plugin and extension
metadata files that make the same skills installable across different agents.

## Repo layout

- [`skills/`](skills/) contains the skill instructions. Each skill lives in its
  own folder with a `SKILL.md` file, such as
  [`skills/search/SKILL.md`](skills/search/SKILL.md).
- [`README.md`](README.md) describes the published skills and installation
  paths.
- [`package.json`](package.json) and
  [`gemini-extension.json`](gemini-extension.json) contain package and extension
  metadata.
- [`.codex-plugin/plugin.json`](.codex-plugin/plugin.json),
  [`.claude-plugin/plugin.json`](.claude-plugin/plugin.json),
  [`.cursor-plugin/plugin.json`](.cursor-plugin/plugin.json),
  [`.agents/plugins/marketplace.json`](.agents/plugins/marketplace.json), and
  [`.opencode/`](.opencode/) support agent-specific installation.

## Before you start

Fork the repo:

1. Click [**Fork**](https://github.com/entireio/skills/fork).
2. Clone your fork:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/skills.git
cd skills
```

Create a descriptive branch from `main`. Use a name that explains the skill or
change you are working on:

```bash
git checkout main
git pull origin main
git checkout -b add-session-review-skill
```

## Adding or changing a skill

Each skill must live at:

```text
skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md
```

Every `SKILL.md` needs YAML front matter with at least:

```yaml
---
name: skill-name
description: A short description of when an agent should use this skill.
---
```

The `name` and `description` fields are required for skill discovery. Missing
metadata can cause installers to skip the skill.

Before writing, read one existing skill that is similar to your change. For
example, if you are adding a provenance skill, read `skills/explain/SKILL.md` or
`skills/what-happened/SKILL.md` first so the new instructions match the repo's
style and level of detail.

Good skill instructions should:

- say when to use the skill and when not to use it
- give the agent a concrete workflow
- name required commands, files, and expected inputs
- describe failure modes and what the agent should tell the user
- avoid secrets, private transcripts, or user-specific local paths
- stay focused on one repeatable workflow instead of becoming a general manual

If you add, remove, rename, or materially change a skill, update `README.md`
when the public list or examples change.

- [`README.md`](README.md)
- [`package.json`](package.json)
- [`gemini-extension.json`](gemini-extension.json)
- plugin metadata like `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`, `.claude-plugin/plugin.json`, `.cursor-plugin/plugin.json`, `.agents/plugins/marketplace.json`, and `.opencode/`

# Contributing to Entire Skills

Thanks for helping improve Entire Skills. This repo is intentionally small: each
skill lives in `skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`, with a few plugin and extension
metadata files that make the same skills installable across different agents.

## Before you start

Fork the repo:

1. Click [**Fork**](https://github.com/entireio/skills/fork).
2. Clone your fork:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/skills.git
cd skills
```

Create a descriptive branch from `main`. Use a name that explains the skill or
change you are working on:

```bash
git checkout main
git pull origin main
git checkout -b add-session-review-skill
```

## Adding or changing a skill

Each skill must live at:

```text
skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md
```

Every `SKILL.md` needs YAML front matter with at least:

```yaml
---
name: skill-name
description: A short description of when an agent should use this skill.
---
```

The `name` and `description` fields are required for skill discovery. Missing
metadata can cause installers to skip the skill.

Before writing, read one existing skill that is similar to your change. For
example, if you are adding a provenance skill, read `skills/explain/SKILL.md` or
`skills/what-happened/SKILL.md` first so the new instructions match the repo's
style and level of detail.

Good skill instructions should:

- say when to use the skill and when not to use it
- give the agent a concrete workflow
- name required commands, files, and expected inputs
- describe failure modes and what the agent should tell the user
- avoid secrets, private transcripts, or user-specific local paths
- stay focused on one repeatable workflow instead of becoming a general manual

If you add, remove, rename, or materially change a skill, update `README.md`
when the public list or examples change.

## Validation

Before opening a PR, run the checks that match your change.

Validate JSON files:

```bash
node -e 'for (const f of ["package.json","gemini-extension.json",".codex-plugin/plugin.json",".claude-plugin/plugin.json",".cursor-plugin/plugin.json",".agents/plugins/marketplace.json"]) JSON.parse(require("fs").readFileSync(f, "utf8"))'
```

Check that the skills installer can discover the repo skills:

```bash
npx -y skills@latest add . --list --yes
```

If you changed the OpenCode plugin shim, check its syntax:

```bash
node --check .opencode/plugins/entire.js
```

For Markdown-only changes, read the rendered Markdown or preview it in GitHub
before submitting.

## Pull requests

Push your branch:

```bash
git push origin add-session-review-skill
```

Open a pull request:

1. Go to your fork on GitHub.
2. Click **Compare & pull request**.
3. Make sure the base repository is `entireio/skills` and the base branch is
   `main`.

In the PR description, include:

- what changed
- why it changed
- which skills or install paths are affected
- what validation you ran

If you changed skill behavior, include a short example prompt that demonstrates
the new or updated behavior.

## Style

- Prefer concise, direct instructions over long explanations.
- Use exact commands when the workflow depends on a command.
- Keep examples generic unless a specific tool or repo is required.
- Do not paste raw session transcripts into skills.
- Do not commit local agent state, logs, temp files, or machine-specific config.

content.text:
I think it’s pretty close. It now feels first-time-contributor friendly and not too maintainer-heavy.

The only thing I’d still change is the **Validation** section. Right now it asks everyone to validate all plugin JSON files and mentions the OpenCode shim. That may feel like “wait, am I supposed to understand all these install paths?” For first-time contributors, I’d make the core check the skill discovery command, then tuck JSON/OpenCode behind “if you changed metadata/code.”

I’d suggest this shape:

```md
## Validation

Before opening a PR, check that the skills installer can discover the repo skills:

```bash
npx -y skills@latest add . --list --yes
```

If you changed JSON metadata, validate the JSON:

...

If you changed the OpenCode plugin shim, check its syntax:

...
```

Tiny wording thing: “which skills or install paths are affected” in the PR section may also be slightly too insider-y. Maybe:

```md
which skill changed, if any
```

Overall: good bones. It’s concise, tells people exactly how to fork/branch/PR, and explains the part that actually matters in this repo: `SKILL.md` metadata and focused workflows.
okay make those changes
I’ll tune validation so the default path is just skill discovery, and simplify the PR checklist wording so it doesn’t imply contributors need to understand install plumbing.

# Contributing to Entire Skills

Thanks for helping improve Entire Skills. This repo is intentionally small: each
skill lives in `skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`, with a few plugin and extension
metadata files that make the same skills installable across different agents.

## Before you start

2. Clone your fork:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/skills.git
cd skills
```

Create a descriptive branch from `main`. Use a name that explains the skill or
change you are working on:

```bash
git checkout main
git pull origin main
git checkout -b add-session-review-skill
```

## Adding or changing a skill

Each skill must live at:

```text
skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md
```

Every `SKILL.md` needs YAML front matter with at least:

```yaml
---
name: skill-name
description: A short description of when an agent should use this skill.
---
```

The `name` and `description` fields are required for skill discovery. Missing
metadata can cause installers to skip the skill.

Before writing, read one existing skill that is similar to your change. For
example, if you are adding a provenance skill, read `skills/explain/SKILL.md` or
`skills/what-happened/SKILL.md` first so the new instructions match the repo's
style and level of detail.

Good skill instructions should:

- say when to use the skill and when not to use it
- give the agent a concrete workflow
- name required commands, files, and expected inputs
- describe failure modes and what the agent should tell the user
- avoid secrets, private transcripts, or user-specific local paths
- stay focused on one repeatable workflow instead of becoming a general manual

If you add, remove, rename, or materially change a skill, update `README.md`
when the public list or examples change.

## Validation

Before opening a PR, check that the skills installer can discover the repo
skills:

```bash
npx -y skills@latest add . --list --yes
```

If you changed JSON metadata, validate the JSON:

```bash
node -e 'for (const f of ["package.json","gemini-extension.json",".codex-plugin/plugin.json",".claude-plugin/plugin.json",".cursor-plugin/plugin.json",".agents/plugins/marketplace.json"]) JSON.parse(require("fs").readFileSync(f, "utf8"))'
```

If you changed the OpenCode plugin shim, check its syntax:

```bash
node --check .opencode/plugins/entire.js
```

For Markdown-only changes, read the rendered Markdown or preview it in GitHub
before submitting.

## Pull requests

Push your branch:

```bash
git push origin add-session-review-skill
```

Open a pull request:

1. Go to your fork on GitHub.
2. Click **Compare & pull request**.
3. Make sure the base repository is `entireio/skills` and the base branch is

In the PR description, include:

- what changed
- why it changed

If you changed skill behavior, include a short example prompt that demonstrates
the new or updated behavior.

## Style

- Prefer concise, direct instructions over long explanations.
- Use exact commands when the workflow depends on a command.

content.text:
Made those edits in [CONTRIBUTING.md](/Users/rizel/Documents/work/skills/CONTRIBUTING.md).

The guide now leads validation with the one check most contributors need:

```bash
npx -y skills@latest add . --list --yes
```

Then JSON/OpenCode checks are conditional. I also changed the PR checklist from “which skills or install paths are affected” to “which skill changed, if any.” Much friendlier.
ok what do we think now

I think it’s in a strong place now.

It does the useful first-time contributor things:
- Gives a clear fork, clone, branch, PR path.
- Explains the one repo-specific gotcha: every `SKILL.md` needs `name` and `description`.
- Keeps quality guidance practical: one repeatable workflow, clear commands, failure modes.
- Makes validation simple by default.
- Avoids maintainer-ish sections about releases, repo layout, and compatibility plumbing.

The only tiny thing I’d consider changing is the intro. This sentence is accurate, but slightly internal:

```md
with a few plugin and extension metadata files that make the same skills installable across different agents.
```

You could leave it. Or make it softer:

```md
This repo is intentionally small: each skill lives in `skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`, and the rest of the repo helps publish those skills for different agents.
```

But overall, yes, it reads like something a first-time contributor could actually follow.
lets open a PR. im okay where it is
I’ll turn the current `CONTRIBUTING.md` work into a PR now. I’ll keep the existing untracked local files out of the commit and only stage the contributor guide.
We’re on `main`, and the only repo change I need to include is the new untracked `CONTRIBUTING.md`. I’m creating a branch now, then I’ll stage just that file.
